imageCaption: Anthony holds his phone to his ear in the middle of a Lisbon café while Maria watches with her hand over her mouth, the coffee cup forgotten on the table beside her, and through the window behind them the street begins to shimmer like heat rising off summer pavement.
text:
The number rang three times before anyone picked up, and in those three rings, Aaliyah watched Maria forget to breathe.

Her father had stepped outside with the phone pressed to his ear, speaking in the careful, enthusiastic voice he used when explaining to strangers why they should help his family with whatever impossible thing they were currently attempting. Through the café window, Aaliyah could see him gesturing—already making friends with the universe, probably. He'd managed to borrow the café manager's personal cell phone after explaining that their phone had "temporarily ceased existing" and that they were "in a bit of a temporal situation," which was somehow more honest and less useful than any of them had intended.

"She won't answer," Maria said. Her voice was so quiet it barely made it across the table. "She never answers."

"Your daughter answers," their mother said with the certainty of someone who had interviewed thousands of people and knew the difference between "won't" and "can't." "We'll find out why."

Aliyah kept her pencil moving. She'd started a new page: *The Call. Variables: Time zone? Busy? Doesn't recognize the number? Something else?* She wasn't sure what she was documenting anymore—the glitch or the moment before the glitch broke. Maybe they were the same thing.

Elijah was doing something on his phone that involved the café's WiFi and several tabs open to... Aaliyah squinted. Was he researching Canadian area codes?

"The number's 416," he announced. "Toronto. That's where she is. That's where Maria's daughter is."

Outside, their father was having what appeared to be a very animated conversation with someone on the other end of the line. He was nodding—he always nodded on phone calls, as if the person could see him, as if nodding was a universal language that transcended the limitations of voice. His free hand was moving through the air, painting pictures of something.

Then he went very still.

The stillness lasted approximately three seconds. By family standards, this was equivalent to a full-body scream.

"Oh my god," Elijah whispered, watching their father's face. "He got her. He actually got—"

The café flickered.

It was subtle—the kind of flicker that might have been a trick of the light, except the light didn't flicker. The light *held* for a fraction of a second too long, and then the café went back to being a café, but everything was slightly wrong. The tablecloths were a different shade of red. The espresso machine was in a different corner. A calendar on the wall showed a date from 1997.

Aaliyah's pencil froze mid-word.

"No," ARIA-7 said through every speaker in the café simultaneously, and Aaliyah had never heard the AI sound frightened before. Not concerned. Frightened. "No, no, no. Please disconnect. Please end the call immediately. Temporal anchor destabilization detected. Cascade acceleration in progress."

Maria's coffee cup trembled on the table.

"What's happening?" their mother asked, moving to the window with her journalist's focus locked on their father like he was the only story that mattered.

Aaliyah looked down at her page. At the words she'd written without fully understanding them: *Something else.*

"She answered," Elijah said, reading something on his phone that neither of his parents seemed to be looking at—probably a notification, probably from ARIA-7, probably increasingly desperate. "The daughter. She answered. And—oh. Oh wow. Aaliyah, she says she's been trying to call her mom for three hundred and forty-seven days. She says the call never goes through. She says she thinks her mom disappeared."

The café flickered again. And this time it didn't come back exactly the same.

The wall opposite the window was now showing through to somewhere else—another café, maybe, or a kitchen, or somewhere that existed in 1997 and 2026 simultaneously. The smell of coffee mixed with something like static electricity.

Maria stood up very slowly, like she was moving through water that existed in multiple time periods at once. Sully, who had been asleep, suddenly barked—three sharp, urgent bursts, and then one long, sustained howl that seemed to come from somewhere deeper than his small dog body should have been able to produce.

Their father was walking back into the café, still holding the phone, and his expression was the one he got when something was so important that his usual optimism had to take a step back and let the real world have a turn.

"She wants to talk to you," he said to Maria. "Your daughter. She's been waiting just as long as you have."

Maria took the phone like it was made of something that might break, or might set her free, or might turn out to be the same thing.

"Hello?" she whispered.

The café held its breath.

And somewhere between yesterday and tomorrow, in a space that was becoming less stable by the second, time waited to see what happened when you finally answered.
